mnemonics.title: MESI States
mnemonics.content: MESI Protocol: Modified (changed), Exclusive (clean, only in one cache), Shared (clean, in multiple caches), Invalid (not present)

mnemonics.title: Cache Operation Steps
mnemonics.content:
HIT: Check tags → Match found → Read data → Update LRU
MISS: Check tags → No match → Select victim → Fetch from memory → Update cache → Read data → Update LRU

mnemonics.title: Write Policies
mnemonics.content: WTTF: Write Through is slow (writes both), Write Back is fast (writes to cache only)

acronyms.usage: Replacement policy that evicts the least recently accessed cache line
Replacement policy that evicts the oldest cache line
Cache coherence protocol for multi-processor systems
Performance metric: Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)
Cache for virtual to physical address translations

visualTips.visualization: Draw cache as a table with columns: Index, Tag, Valid, Dirty, Data. Show how address bits split into Tag, Index, and Offset fields.
Create a decision tree: Request → Check Valid+Tag Match → Yes (Hit) → No (Miss) → Fetch from Memory → Update Cache → Return Data
Side-by-side diagrams: Write-Through (Cache → Memory simultaneously) vs Write-Back (Cache → Memory on eviction only)

analogies.analogy: Think of cache as your desk (fast access) and main memory as a library (slower). The cache controller is like a librarian who decides what books to keep on your desk.
Cache miss is like asking a friend for a book they don't have - they must go to the library (main memory), taking much longer than if they had it already.
LRU is like keeping your most-used items on your desk while moving rarely used items to storage. FIFO is like a revolving desk drawer.
A write buffer is like a delivery truck - the processor puts packages (writes) in the truck to deliver to main memory while continuing to work.
